lines.text: Section twelve. Upcoming technologies that will shape synthetic biology next.

The textbook closes the foundational-technologies chapter with a brief look ahead at the technologies that, as of the revised edition's writing in 2015, were poised to define the next generation of synthetic biology.
Some of these have since matured considerably; others remain frontier work.
Let me walk through each in turn.
First, high-performance computation enabling more complex modelling.
The authors specifically call out multi-scale modelling — combining models at different scales (molecular, cellular, population) into a single coherent simulation.
Each level of model has its own dynamics — protein folding at picoseconds, gene expression at minutes, cell growth at hours, evolution at days or weeks — and integrating them is computationally hard.
More compute makes it tractable.
Combined with better part characterisation data, this allows for higher-fidelity predictive modelling, and ultimately for the BioCAD vision we discussed in chapter two to become operational.

The authors describe the end-state explicitly: a drag-and-drop CAD environment for synthetic biology, in which DNA sequences of parts or devices are virtual modules, their function under varying conditions can be simulated, and an optimised design produces a parts list and full DNA sequence for synthesis along with instructions for assembly — by hand or by lab robotics.
Such CAD environments exist in mature engineering disciplines like microchip design; synthetic biology's are still developing.
The textbook gives Cello, the genetic circuit design program from MIT, as a then-emerging example.
Second, computational sequence-to-function models that produce parts on demand.
The example the textbook highlights is the RBS Calculator from 2009 by Salis, Mirsky, and Voigt — which we already discussed in chapter one's section on translational control.
The model takes a desired translation initiation rate and a sequence context, and returns a DNA sequence that, when synthesised, should produce the requested behaviour.
If you can predict-and-synthesise instead of search-and-pick, you no longer need a registry for that class of parts — every needed part can be generated on demand.

This is a kind of design freedom that no traditional engineering discipline has access to, because in electronics you cannot synthesise an arbitrary transistor — but in biology, with cheap DNA synthesis, you can.
Third, gene synthesis continuing to scale.
Costs continue to fall, lengths continue to grow, and turnaround times continue to shrink.
Codon-use models are getting more sophisticated, producing genes with more predictable expression levels in their target hosts.

And a new approach — gene synthesis from microarray-printed oligonucleotides — promises to drop costs even further by exploiting the dramatically lower cost-per-base of microarray-format oligonucleotide synthesis compared to conventional methods.
Fourth, miniaturisation via microfluidics.
Microfluidic devices are custom-fabricated chips with channels and chambers at the micrometre scale, in which thousands of parallel laboratory reactions can be carried out in tiny volumes.
For synthetic biology, microfluidics offers two big wins.
One: massively parallel characterisation of parts and devices, with much smaller reagent consumption than conventional well-plate experiments.

Two: precise control of the local environment of each engineered cell — allowing experiments like the one the authors cite, where the dynamics of oscillating engineered cells could be monitored separately for thousands of cells, each with its own controlled stimulus.
Microfluidics is one of the technologies most likely to expand both the throughput and the precision of synthetic biology experimentation over the coming decade.
Each of these upcoming technologies points in the same direction: making synthetic biology cheaper, faster, more reproducible, more predictive, and ultimately more accessible.

The authors close by emphasising that the goal is not just to advance the science, but to industrialise it — to turn synthetic biology into a predictable technology platform that can be deployed in commercial settings to solve real-world problems.
Whether that goal is exactly the right one, and whether it can be achieved responsibly, is the subject of chapter nine.
But the technological trajectory is unmistakable, and chapter three has now laid out what each of the foundational pieces looks like.
End of chapter three.
